1	# Quack
2	
3	This repository is based on https://github.com/duckdb/extension-template, check it out if you want to build and ship your own DuckDB extension.
4	
5	---
6	
7	This extension, Quack, allow you to ... <extension_goal>.
8	
9	
10	## Building
11	
12	### Build steps
13	Now to build the extension, run:
14	```sh
15	make
16	```
17	The main binaries that will be built are:
18	```sh
19	./build/release/duckdb
20	./build/release/test/unittest
21	./build/release/extension/quack/quack.duckdb_extension
22	```
23	- `duckdb` is the binary for the duckdb shell with the extension code automatically loaded.
24	- `unittest` is the test runner of duckdb. Again, the extension is already linked into the binary.
25	- `quack.duckdb_extension` is the loadable binary as it would be distributed.
26	
27	## Running the extension
28	To run the extension code, simply start the shell with `./build/release/duckdb`.
29	
30	Now we can use the features from the extension directly in DuckDB. The template contains a single scalar function `quack()` that takes a string arguments and returns a string:
31	```
32	D select quack('Jane') as result;
33	┌───────────────┐
34	│    result     │
35	│    varchar    │
36	├───────────────┤
37	│ Quack Jane 🐥 │
38	└───────────────┘
39	```
40	
41	## Running the tests
42	Different tests can be created for DuckDB extensions. The primary way of testing DuckDB extensions should be the SQL tests in `./test/sql`. These SQL tests can be run using:
43	```sh
44	make test
45	```
46	
47	### Installing the deployed binaries
48	To install your extension binaries from S3, you will need to do two things. Firstly, DuckDB should be launched with the
49	`allow_unsigned_extensions` option set to true. How to set this will depend on the client you're using. Some examples:
50	
51	CLI:
52	```shell
53	duckdb -unsigned
54	```
55	
56	Python:
57	```python
58	con = duckdb.connect(':memory:', config={'allow_unsigned_extensions' : 'true'})
59	```
60	
61	NodeJS:
62	```js
63	db = new duckdb.Database(':memory:', {"allow_unsigned_extensions": "true"});
64	```
65	
66	Secondly, you will need to set the repository endpoint in DuckDB to the HTTP url of your bucket + version of the extension
67	you want to install. To do this run the following SQL query in DuckDB:
68	```sql
69	SET custom_extension_repository='bucket.s3.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/<your_extension_name>/latest';
70	```
71	Note that the `/latest` path will allow you to install the latest extension version available for your current version of
72	DuckDB. To specify a specific version, you can pass the version instead.
73	
74	After running these steps, you can install and load your extension using the regular INSTALL/LOAD commands in DuckDB:
75	```sql
76	INSTALL quack;
77	LOAD quack;
78	```
79	
80	## Setting up CLion
81	
82	### Opening project
83	Configuring CLion with this extension requires a little work. Firstly, make sure that the DuckDB submodule is available.
84	Then make sure to open `./duckdb/CMakeLists.txt` (so not the top level `CMakeLists.txt` file from this repo) as a project in CLion.
85	Now to fix your project path go to `tools->CMake->Change Project Root`([docs](https://www.jetbrains.com/help/clion/change-project-root-directory.html)) to set the project root to the root dir of this repo.
86	
87	### Debugging
88	To set up debugging in CLion, there are two simple steps required. Firstly, in `CLion -> Settings / Preferences -> Build, Execution, Deploy -> CMake` you will need to add the desired builds (e.g. Debug, Release, RelDebug, etc). There's different ways to configure this, but the easiest is to leave all empty, except the `build path`, which needs to be set to `../build/{build type}`, and CMake Options to which the following flag should be added, with the path to the extension CMakeList:
89	
90	```
91	-DDUCKDB_EXTENSION_CONFIGS=<path_to_the_exentension_CMakeLists.txt>
92	```
93	
94	The second step is to configure the unittest runner as a run/debug configuration. To do this, go to `Run -> Edit Configurations` and click `+ -> Cmake Application`. The target and executable should be `unittest`. This will run all the DuckDB tests. To specify only running the extension specific tests, add `--test-dir ../../.. [sql]` to the `Program Arguments`. Note that it is recommended to use the `unittest` executable for testing/development within CLion. The actual DuckDB CLI currently does not reliably work as a run target in CLion.
95

file.content:
# Quack

This repository is based on https://github.com/duckdb/extension-template, check it out if you want to build and ship your own DuckDB extension.

---

This extension, Quack, allow you to ... <extension_goal>.


## Building

### Build steps
Now to build the extension, run:
```sh
make
```
The main binaries that will be built are:
```sh
./build/release/duckdb
./build/release/test/unittest
./build/release/extension/quack/quack.duckdb_extension
```
- `duckdb` is the binary for the duckdb shell with the extension code automatically loaded.
- `unittest` is the test runner of duckdb. Again, the extension is already linked into the binary.
- `quack.duckdb_extension` is the loadable binary as it would be distributed.

## Running the extension
To run the extension code, simply start the shell with `./build/release/duckdb`.

Now we can use the features from the extension directly in DuckDB. The template contains a single scalar function `quack()` that takes a string arguments and returns a string:
```
D select quack('Jane') as result;
┌───────────────┐
│    result     │
│    varchar    │
├───────────────┤
│ Quack Jane 🐥 │
└───────────────┘
```

## Running the tests
Different tests can be created for DuckDB extensions. The primary way of testing DuckDB extensions should be the SQL tests in `./test/sql`. These SQL tests can be run using:
```sh
make test
```

### Installing the deployed binaries
To install your extension binaries from S3, you will need to do two things. Firstly, DuckDB should be launched with the
`allow_unsigned_extensions` option set to true. How to set this will depend on the client you're using. Some examples:

CLI:
```shell
duckdb -unsigned
```

Python:
```python
con = duckdb.connect(':memory:', config={'allow_unsigned_extensions' : 'true'})
```

NodeJS:
```js
db = new duckdb.Database(':memory:', {"allow_unsigned_extensions": "true"});
```

Secondly, you will need to set the repository endpoint in DuckDB to the HTTP url of your bucket + version of the extension
you want to install. To do this run the following SQL query in DuckDB:
```sql
SET custom_extension_repository='bucket.s3.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/<your_extension_name>/latest';
```
Note that the `/latest` path will allow you to install the latest extension version available for your current version of
DuckDB. To specify a specific version, you can pass the version instead.

After running these steps, you can install and load your extension using the regular INSTALL/LOAD commands in DuckDB:
```sql
INSTALL quack;
LOAD quack;
```

## Setting up CLion

### Opening project
Configuring CLion with this extension requires a little work. Firstly, make sure that the DuckDB submodule is available.
Then make sure to open `./duckdb/CMakeLists.txt` (so not the top level `CMakeLists.txt` file from this repo) as a project in CLion.
Now to fix your project path go to `tools->CMake->Change Project Root`([docs](https://www.jetbrains.com/help/clion/change-project-root-directory.html)) to set the project root to the root dir of this repo.

### Debugging
To set up debugging in CLion, there are two simple steps required. Firstly, in `CLion -> Settings / Preferences -> Build, Execution, Deploy -> CMake` you will need to add the desired builds (e.g. Debug, Release, RelDebug, etc). There's different ways to configure this, but the easiest is to leave all empty, except the `build path`, which needs to be set to `../build/{build type}`, and CMake Options to which the following flag should be added, with the path to the extension CMakeList:

```
-DDUCKDB_EXTENSION_CONFIGS=<path_to_the_exentension_CMakeLists.txt>
```

The second step is to configure the unittest runner as a run/debug configuration. To do this, go to `Run -> Edit Configurations` and click `+ -> Cmake Application`. The target and executable should be `unittest`. This will run all the DuckDB tests. To specify only running the extension specific tests, add `--test-dir ../../.. [sql]` to the `Program Arguments`. Note that it is recommended to use the `unittest` executable for testing/development within CLion. The actual DuckDB CLI currently does not reliably work as a run target in CLion.

1	# name: test/sql/jq.test
2	# description: test jq extension
3	# group: [sql]
4	
5	# Before we load the extension, this will fail
6	statement error
7	SELECT jq('{}', '.');
8	----
9	Catalog Error
10	
11	require jq
12	
13	# Single-value filter
14	query I
15	SELECT jq('{"a": 1, "b": [10,20,30]}', '.b[1]');
16	----
17	20
18	
19	# Filter producing multiple results is wrapped in a JSON array
20	query I
21	SELECT jq('{"a": 1, "b": [10,20,30]}', '.b[]');
22	----
23	[10,20,30]
24	
25	# Missing field returns JSON null (a single result)
26	query I
27	SELECT jq('{"a": 1}', '.missing');
28	----
29	null
30	
31	# Built-in jq functions work (oniguruma-backed filters too)
32	query I
33	SELECT jq('{"name": "alice"}', '.name | ascii_upcase');
34	----
35	"ALICE"
36	
37	# Zero results from the filter become SQL NULL
38	query II
39	SELECT jq('1', 'empty') AS v, jq('1', 'empty') IS NULL AS is_null;
40	----
41	NULL	true
42	
43	# Per-row application across a column of JSON inputs
44	query I
45	SELECT jq(j, '.x') FROM (VALUES ('{"x":1}'), ('{"x":2}'), ('{"x":3}')) t(j);
46	----
47	1
48	2
49	3
50	
51	# Invalid jq syntax is rejected at bind time
52	statement error
53	SELECT jq('1', '.[');
54	----
55	failed to compile filter
56	
57	# Filter argument must be a constant expression
58	statement error
59	WITH t(j, f) AS (VALUES ('1', '.')) SELECT jq(j, f) FROM t;
60	----
61	jq filter must be a constant expression
62

# name: test/sql/jq.test
# description: test jq extension
# group: [sql]

# Before we load the extension, this will fail
statement error
SELECT jq('{}', '.');
----
Catalog Error

require jq

# Single-value filter
query I
SELECT jq('{"a": 1, "b": [10,20,30]}', '.b[1]');
----
20

# Filter producing multiple results is wrapped in a JSON array
query I
SELECT jq('{"a": 1, "b": [10,20,30]}', '.b[]');
----
[10,20,30]

# Missing field returns JSON null (a single result)
query I
SELECT jq('{"a": 1}', '.missing');
----
null

# Built-in jq functions work (oniguruma-backed filters too)
query I
SELECT jq('{"name": "alice"}', '.name | ascii_upcase');
----
"ALICE"

# Zero results from the filter become SQL NULL
query II
SELECT jq('1', 'empty') AS v, jq('1', 'empty') IS NULL AS is_null;
----
NULL	true

# Per-row application across a column of JSON inputs
query I
SELECT jq(j, '.x') FROM (VALUES ('{"x":1}'), ('{"x":2}'), ('{"x":3}')) t(j);
----
1
2
3

# Invalid jq syntax is rejected at bind time
statement error
SELECT jq('1', '.[');
----
failed to compile filter

# Filter argument must be a constant expression
statement error
WITH t(j, f) AS (VALUES ('1', '.')) SELECT jq(j, f) FROM t;
----
jq filter must be a constant expression

content.content:
1	cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.5)
2	
3	set(TARGET_NAME jq)
4	set(EXTENSION_NAME ${TARGET_NAME}_extension)
5	set(LOADABLE_EXTENSION_NAME ${TARGET_NAME}_loadable_extension)
6	
7	project(${TARGET_NAME})
8	
9	set(CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD "17" CACHE STRING "C++ standard to enforce")
10	set(CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD_REQUIRED ON)
11	
12	include_directories(src/include)
13	
14	# --- libjq (built from the ./jq submodule via its own autotools build) ---
15	include(ExternalProject)
16	
17	set(JQ_SRC_DIR     ${CMAKE_CURRENT_SOURCE_DIR}/jq)
18	set(JQ_BUILD_DIR   ${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}/jq-build)
19	set(JQ_INSTALL_DIR ${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}/jq-install)
20	set(JQ_STATIC_LIB    ${JQ_INSTALL_DIR}/lib/libjq.a)
21	set(ONIG_STATIC_LIB  ${JQ_INSTALL_DIR}/lib/libonig.a)
22	
23	ExternalProject_Add(jq_external
24	  SOURCE_DIR        ${JQ_SRC_DIR}
25	  BINARY_DIR        ${JQ_BUILD_DIR}
26	  INSTALL_DIR       ${JQ_INSTALL_DIR}
27	  CONFIGURE_COMMAND ${CMAKE_COMMAND} -E chdir ${JQ_SRC_DIR} autoreconf -i
28	            COMMAND ${JQ_SRC_DIR}/configure
29	                      --prefix=${JQ_INSTALL_DIR}
30	                      --disable-shared --enable-static --with-pic
31	                      --disable-maintainer-mode --disable-docs
32	                      --with-oniguruma=builtin
33	  BUILD_COMMAND      ${CMAKE_MAKE_PROGRAM}
34	  INSTALL_COMMAND    ${CMAKE_MAKE_PROGRAM} install
35	  BUILD_BYPRODUCTS   ${JQ_STATIC_LIB} ${ONIG_STATIC_LIB}
36	)
37	
38	# Make headers visible at configure time (the dir exists after install; create
39	# it now so include_directories doesn't warn on first run).
40	file(MAKE_DIRECTORY ${JQ_INSTALL_DIR}/include)
41	
42	add_library(libjq STATIC IMPORTED GLOBAL)
43	add_dependencies(libjq jq_external)
44	set_target_properties(libjq PROPERTIES
45	  IMPORTED_LOCATION             ${JQ_STATIC_LIB}
46	  INTERFACE_INCLUDE_DIRECTORIES ${JQ_INSTALL_DIR}/include
47	)
48	
49	add_library(libonig STATIC IMPORTED GLOBAL)
50	add_dependencies(libonig jq_external)
51	set_target_properties(libonig PROPERTIES
52	  IMPORTED_LOCATION ${ONIG_STATIC_LIB}
53	)
54	
55	set(EXTENSION_SOURCES src/jq_extension.cpp)
56	
57	build_static_extension(${TARGET_NAME} ${EXTENSION_SOURCES})
58	build_loadable_extension(${TARGET_NAME} " " ${EXTENSION_SOURCES})
59	
60	# Link libjq (with its bundled oniguruma) in both the static library and the loadable extension
61	target_link_libraries(${EXTENSION_NAME}          libjq libonig)
62	target_link_libraries(${LOADABLE_EXTENSION_NAME} libjq libonig)
63	
64	install(
65	  TARGETS ${EXTENSION_NAME}
66	  EXPORT "${DUCKDB_EXPORT_SET}"
67	  LIBRARY DESTINATION "${INSTALL_LIB_DIR}"
68	  ARCHIVE DESTINATION "${INSTALL_LIB_DIR}")
69

file.content:
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.5)

set(TARGET_NAME jq)
set(EXTENSION_NAME ${TARGET_NAME}_extension)
set(LOADABLE_EXTENSION_NAME ${TARGET_NAME}_loadable_extension)

project(${TARGET_NAME})

set(CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD "17" CACHE STRING "C++ standard to enforce")
set(CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD_REQUIRED ON)

include_directories(src/include)

# --- libjq (built from the ./jq submodule via its own autotools build) ---
include(ExternalProject)

set(JQ_SRC_DIR     ${CMAKE_CURRENT_SOURCE_DIR}/jq)
set(JQ_BUILD_DIR   ${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}/jq-build)
set(JQ_INSTALL_DIR ${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}/jq-install)
set(JQ_STATIC_LIB    ${JQ_INSTALL_DIR}/lib/libjq.a)
set(ONIG_STATIC_LIB  ${JQ_INSTALL_DIR}/lib/libonig.a)

ExternalProject_Add(jq_external
  SOURCE_DIR        ${JQ_SRC_DIR}
  BINARY_DIR        ${JQ_BUILD_DIR}
  INSTALL_DIR       ${JQ_INSTALL_DIR}
  CONFIGURE_COMMAND ${CMAKE_COMMAND} -E chdir ${JQ_SRC_DIR} autoreconf -i
            COMMAND ${JQ_SRC_DIR}/configure
                      --prefix=${JQ_INSTALL_DIR}
                      --disable-shared --enable-static --with-pic
                      --disable-maintainer-mode --disable-docs
                      --with-oniguruma=builtin
  BUILD_COMMAND      ${CMAKE_MAKE_PROGRAM}
  INSTALL_COMMAND    ${CMAKE_MAKE_PROGRAM} install
  BUILD_BYPRODUCTS   ${JQ_STATIC_LIB} ${ONIG_STATIC_LIB}
)

# Make headers visible at configure time (the dir exists after install; create
# it now so include_directories doesn't warn on first run).
file(MAKE_DIRECTORY ${JQ_INSTALL_DIR}/include)

add_library(libjq STATIC IMPORTED GLOBAL)
add_dependencies(libjq jq_external)
set_target_properties(libjq PROPERTIES
  IMPORTED_LOCATION             ${JQ_STATIC_LIB}
  INTERFACE_INCLUDE_DIRECTORIES ${JQ_INSTALL_DIR}/include
)

add_library(libonig STATIC IMPORTED GLOBAL)
add_dependencies(libonig jq_external)
set_target_properties(libonig PROPERTIES
  IMPORTED_LOCATION ${ONIG_STATIC_LIB}
)

set(EXTENSION_SOURCES src/jq_extension.cpp)

build_static_extension(${TARGET_NAME} ${EXTENSION_SOURCES})
build_loadable_extension(${TARGET_NAME} " " ${EXTENSION_SOURCES})

# Link libjq (with its bundled oniguruma) in both the static library and the loadable extension
target_link_libraries(${EXTENSION_NAME}          libjq libonig)
target_link_libraries(${LOADABLE_EXTENSION_NAME} libjq libonig)

install(
  TARGETS ${EXTENSION_NAME}
  EXPORT "${DUCKDB_EXPORT_SET}"
  LIBRARY DESTINATION "${INSTALL_LIB_DIR}"
  ARCHIVE DESTINATION "${INSTALL_LIB_DIR}")

content.thinking: Now I have a clear picture of what this extension does. The `jq` extension provides a scalar function that takes JSON text and a constant filter expression, then applies the jq filter and wraps any multiple results. I'll rewrite the README to focus on this core functionality while keeping the practical sections like building and testing.

content.text: I have what I need. The extension provides a `jq(json, filter)` scalar function that applies a jq filter to a JSON string. Let me rewrite the README.

input.content:
# duckdb-jq

A DuckDB extension that exposes [jq](https://jqlang.org/) as a scalar SQL function, letting you run jq filters against JSON values directly in SQL queries.

The extension links jq (with its bundled oniguruma regex engine) statically, so no external `jq` binary is required at runtime.

This repository is based on https://github.com/duckdb/extension-template.

## Usage

The extension registers a single scalar function:

```
jq(json_text VARCHAR, filter VARCHAR) -> JSON
```

- `json_text` — a JSON document as a string. Evaluated per row.
- `filter` — a jq filter. Must be a **constant expression**; it's compiled once at bind time and reused across rows.

Result semantics:

- A filter producing a single value returns that value as JSON.
- A filter producing multiple values is wrapped into a JSON array.
- A filter producing zero values (e.g. `empty`) returns SQL `NULL`.
- An invalid filter is rejected at bind time; invalid input JSON raises at execution time.

### Examples

```sql
-- Pick a single value
SELECT jq('{"a": 1, "b": [10,20,30]}', '.b[1]');
-- 20

-- Multi-result filters are wrapped in a JSON array
SELECT jq('{"a": 1, "b": [10,20,30]}', '.b[]');
-- [10,20,30]

-- Built-in jq functions, including oniguruma-backed ones
SELECT jq('{"name": "alice"}', '.name | ascii_upcase');
-- "ALICE"

-- Zero results become SQL NULL
SELECT jq('1', 'empty') IS NULL;
-- true

-- Apply a fixed filter across a column of JSON values
SELECT jq(j, '.x') FROM (VALUES ('{"x":1}'), ('{"x":2}')) t(j);
-- 1
-- 2
```

## Building

The jq source lives in the `./jq` submodule and is built via its own autotools build. Make sure submodules are initialized:

```sh
git submodule update --init --recursive
```

Then build:

```sh
make
```

The main binaries produced are:

```
./build/release/duckdb
./build/release/test/unittest
./build/release/extension/jq/jq.duckdb_extension
```

- `duckdb` is the DuckDB shell with the extension preloaded.
- `unittest` is the DuckDB test runner with the extension linked in.
- `jq.duckdb_extension` is the loadable binary as it would be distributed.

## Running the extension

Start the shell with the extension preloaded:

```sh
./build/release/duckdb
```

Then use `jq(...)` directly in SQL — see the examples above.

## Running the tests

SQL tests live in `./test/sql`. Run them with:

```sh
make test
```

## Installing the deployed binaries

To install the extension binary from S3, DuckDB must be launched with `allow_unsigned_extensions` set to true. Some examples:

CLI:
```sh
duckdb -unsigned
```

Python:
```python
con = duckdb.connect(':memory:', config={'allow_unsigned_extensions' : 'true'})
```

NodeJS:
```js
db = new duckdb.Database(':memory:', {"allow_unsigned_extensions": "true"});
```

Then point DuckDB at the repository hosting the build:

```sql
SET custom_extension_repository='bucket.s3.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/jq/latest';
```

`/latest` resolves to the latest build for your current DuckDB version; substitute a specific version if needed.

```sql
INSTALL jq;
LOAD jq;
```

## Setting up CLion

### Opening project
Configuring CLion with this extension requires a little work. Firstly, make sure that the DuckDB submodule is available. Then make sure to open `./duckdb/CMakeLists.txt` (not the top-level `CMakeLists.txt` from this repo) as a project in CLion. To fix the project path, go to `tools->CMake->Change Project Root` ([docs](https://www.jetbrains.com/help/clion/change-project-root-directory.html)) and set the project root to the root dir of this repo.

### Debugging
In `CLion -> Settings / Preferences -> Build, Execution, Deploy -> CMake`, add the desired builds (Debug, Release, etc). Leave most fields empty, set `build path` to `../build/{build type}`, and add the following CMake option pointing at this extension's CMakeLists:

```
-DDUCKDB_EXTENSION_CONFIGS=<path_to_the_extension_CMakeLists.txt>
```

Then configure the unittest runner as a run/debug configuration: `Run -> Edit Configurations`, `+ -> CMake Application`, with target and executable `unittest`. To run only the extension tests, add `--test-dir ../../.. [sql]` to `Program Arguments`. Use the `unittest` executable for testing within CLion — the DuckDB CLI doesn't currently work reliably as a CLion run target.
